stem: Your support agent has two tools with nearly identical descriptions: `analyze_content` ("Analyze content and return insights") and `analyze_document` ("Analyze a document and return insights"). The model routes requests to the wrong one roughly half the time. What is the single most effective fix?

options.A: Rewrite the descriptions so each clearly states its distinct purpose, inputs, outputs, and when to use it versus the other
options.B: Delete one of the tools and route everything through the survivor
options.C: Lower the model temperature so tool selection becomes more deterministic
options.D: Force `tool_choice: any` so the model is required to pick one of them

explanation: Tool descriptions are the PRIMARY mechanism the model uses to select tools. Overlapping, vague descriptions cause misrouting. Differentiating each description (purpose, inputs, outputs, when-to-use-vs-alternatives), and ideally renaming to remove overlap (e.g. `extract_web_results`), fixes selection accuracy.

distractors.B: Deleting a tool may discard a genuinely needed capability; the real defect is description overlap, not tool count.
distractors.C: Temperature does not fix ambiguous semantics; the model still cannot tell the tools apart because the descriptions are interchangeable.
distractors.D: `tool_choice: any` forces a call but does nothing to help the model choose the CORRECT one between two ambiguous options.

stem: Your MCP server exposes the company return policy, product SKU catalog, and shipping-zone reference tables so the agent can reason from them. It also exposes the ability to issue a refund. Which mapping to MCP primitives is correct?

options.A: Expose the policy, catalog, and reference tables as resources; expose refund issuance as a tool
options.B: Expose all four as tools, since they all touch the same backend
options.C: Expose all four as resources, since the agent only reads from them
options.D: Expose the policy and catalog as tools and refund issuance as a resource

explanation: Resources are application-controlled, read-only reference data the model reasons FROM (catalogs, policies, schemas). Tools are model-controlled ACTIONS with side effects. Issuing a refund is an action, so it must be a tool; the read-only reference data should be resources.

distractors.B: Modeling read-only reference data as tools forces exploratory tool calls and misuses the model-controlled action primitive for static data.
distractors.C: Issuing a refund is an action with side effects, not read-only data; it cannot be a resource.
distractors.D: This reverses the model entirely: read-only data becomes actions and an action becomes read-only data.

stem: An MCP tool definition omits the `annotations` block entirely. Given the MCP spec's default annotation values, how will a host most reasonably treat this tool?

options.A: As invalid, because annotations are required for every tool
options.B: As read-only and idempotent, because omitting annotations signals a safe tool
options.C: As identical to a tool with all annotations explicitly set to false
options.D: As potentially destructive and interacting with external entities, because the defaults assume the worst case

explanation: The default annotation values assume worst case: `destructiveHint` defaults to true and `openWorldHint` defaults to true (while `readOnlyHint` and `idempotentHint` default to false). So an un-annotated tool is treated as potentially destructive and open-world — "dangerous until proven safe."

distractors.A: Annotations are optional hints, not required fields; their absence does not make the tool definition invalid.
distractors.B: This inverts the defaults; `readOnlyHint` defaults to false and `idempotentHint` defaults to false, so omission does not signal safety.
distractors.C: Setting all hints to false differs from defaults — `destructiveHint` and `openWorldHint` default to true, not false.

stem: Your team wants a Slack MCP server and a PostgreSQL MCP server available to everyone who clones the repo, with credentials supplied per-developer via environment variables and never committed. Where should this configuration live?

options.A: In the project's `CLAUDE.md` so the instructions travel with the repo
options.B: In each developer's user-scoped `~/.claude.json`
options.C: In a project-scoped `.mcp.json` committed to the repo, using `${VAR}` expansion for credentials
options.D: In a `.mcp.json` with credentials hardcoded, added to `.gitignore`

explanation: Project-scoped `.mcp.json` is git-tracked and shared with everyone who clones the repo. Environment variable expansion (`${VAR}`) keeps secrets out of the committed file while still letting each developer supply their own credentials.

distractors.A: CLAUDE.md holds instructions, not MCP server configuration; server config belongs in `.mcp.json` or `~/.claude.json`.
distractors.B: User scope is per-machine and not shared via the repo; every developer would have to configure it manually and it would not travel with a clone.
distractors.D: Gitignoring the file defeats the goal of sharing it; the correct pattern is to commit `.mcp.json` and externalize secrets with `${VAR}`.

stem: In `.mcp.json`, a server URL is written as `"url": "${API_BASE_URL}/mcp"`. A developer who has not set `API_BASE_URL` in their environment tries to start Claude Code. What happens, and what one-character-class change would make it resilient?

options.A: It silently uses an empty string for the URL; no change is needed
options.B: Expansion fails because the variable is unset; using `${API_BASE_URL:-https://api.example.com}` supplies a default when unset
options.C: It prompts the developer interactively for the value; switch to `${?API_BASE_URL}` to suppress the prompt
options.D: It falls back to localhost automatically; add `${API_BASE_URL!localhost}` to override

explanation: `${VAR}` expands to the variable's value and FAILS if the variable is unset. The `${VAR:-default}` form substitutes the provided default when the variable is unset, making the config resilient to missing environment variables.

distractors.A: `${VAR}` does not silently become empty; an unset variable causes expansion to fail.
distractors.C: There is no `${?VAR}` interactive-prompt syntax in `.mcp.json` expansion; the supported forms are `${VAR}` and `${VAR:-default}`.
distractors.D: There is no automatic localhost fallback and no `${VAR!default}` syntax; the default form is `${VAR:-default}`.

stem: A customer asks the agent to refund $750. Company policy caps agent-initiated refunds at $500; anything higher must go through a manager workflow. The `process_refund` MCP tool detects the violation. How should it report this so the agent behaves correctly?

options.A: Return a JSON-RPC protocol error with code -32603 so the framework handles it
options.B: Return `isError: true` with `isRetryable: false` and a customer-friendly explanation directing the manager-approval workflow
options.C: Return `isError: true` with `errorCategory: "transient"` and `isRetryable: true` so the agent retries
options.D: Return an empty success result so the agent moves on without refunding

explanation: A policy cap is a business-rule violation: not retryable (retrying the same $750 request will always fail) and visible to the model so it can choose an alternative workflow. Use `isError: true` with `isRetryable: false` and a clear, customer-friendly message pointing to the manager-approval path.

distractors.A: Protocol errors are not seen by the model, so the agent cannot reason about the violation or offer an alternative; business errors belong in `isError`.
distractors.C: Marking a business violation as transient/retryable causes the agent to pointlessly retry an action that will never succeed.
distractors.D: An empty success result silently suppresses the failure and may mislead the agent into thinking the refund was processed.

stem: An MCP client sends a request naming a tool that the server does not expose (a typo in the tool name). At which layer of MCP's two-layer error model does this surface, and can the model reason about it?

options.A: As a JSON-RPC protocol error in the `error` field; the model generally does not see it
options.B: As a silently dropped message with no error at all
options.C: As both a protocol error and an `isError` result simultaneously
options.D: As a tool execution error (`isError: true` in the result); the model sees it and can recover

explanation: Unknown tool names, invalid arguments, and server-level failures are protocol errors and go in the JSON-RPC `error` field (e.g. code -32602). The model does NOT see protocol errors. Only tool-execution errors returned in the `result` with `isError: true` are visible to the model for recovery reasoning.

distractors.B: The protocol returns a structured JSON-RPC error; the message is not silently dropped.
distractors.C: An unknown tool is rejected at the protocol layer before execution; it does not also produce a result-level `isError`.
distractors.D: `isError` is for execution-time failures of a valid tool call, not for an unknown tool name, which is rejected at the protocol layer.

stem: A document-analysis subagent's `fetch_source` tool hits a 30-second network timeout. The subagent returns `{"status": "error", "message": "failed"}` to the coordinator, which immediately re-issues the identical request and times out again. What is the core problem with this error reporting?

options.A: The error should have been a protocol error so the coordinator's framework retries automatically
options.B: The subagent should have returned an empty success result to avoid alarming the coordinator
options.C: The error lacks structured context — failure type, what was attempted, any partial results, and alternative approaches — so the coordinator cannot make an intelligent recovery decision
options.D: The coordinator should never retry; subagent failures must always escalate to a human

explanation: A generic "failed" status hides the information the coordinator needs to recover well: the failure type (transient timeout), what was attempted, any partial results, and possible alternatives. With structured context the coordinator could, for example, back off, try an alternate source, or proceed with partial data instead of blindly retrying.

distractors.A: Subagent execution failures are tool-execution errors the model should see, not protocol errors hidden from it; framework auto-retry would not add the missing context either.
distractors.B: Reporting a failure as empty success suppresses the error and prevents any recovery — an explicit anti-pattern.
distractors.D: Retrying transient failures is legitimate; the issue is the missing context, not retrying itself, and not every failure warrants human escalation.

stem: A `search_orders` tool returns `{"results": []}` in two different situations: (1) the orders database was unreachable, and (2) the query ran successfully but the customer genuinely has no matching orders. The agent treats both the same and tells the customer "you have no orders." What is the right design correction?

options.A: Always retry on an empty result, since empty usually means a transient failure
options.B: Return `isError: true` for both so the agent never tells a customer they have no orders
options.C: Distinguish the two: return `isError: true` with a transient/retryable category for the access failure, and a normal successful empty result for the valid no-matches case
options.D: Return a normal empty result for both and let the agent infer the difference from latency

explanation: An access failure (database unreachable) is fundamentally different from a valid empty result (query succeeded, zero matches). The access failure should be an `isError: true` transient/retryable error so the agent can retry or escalate; the genuine no-matches case is a successful empty result the agent can report truthfully.

distractors.A: Retrying every empty result wastes effort on legitimate no-match queries and still conflates the two cases.
distractors.B: Marking a valid empty result as an error prevents the agent from truthfully telling customers they have no matching orders.
distractors.D: Latency is an unreliable signal; the tool must explicitly distinguish failure from valid-empty rather than forcing the agent to guess.

stem: Your team needs Claude Code to query an internal, proprietary patient-records system in a HIPAA-regulated environment, with full audit logging and in-house control over data access. A popular community MCP server exists for a generic SQL database but not for this system. What is the appropriate choice?

options.A: Fork the community server but leave its code unreviewed to ship faster
options.B: Build a custom MCP server tailored to the proprietary system, with the required compliance, audit, and access controls
options.C: Use the community SQL MCP server and point it at the patient-records database to save time
options.D: Avoid MCP entirely and paste records into the prompt manually

explanation: Proprietary internal systems and regulated industries (healthcare/finance) with custom business logic, audit requirements, and in-house security control are the canonical case for building a CUSTOM MCP server. Community servers are best for standard integrations matching the common case.

distractors.A: Using unreviewed community code in a regulated, sensitive context is a security and compliance anti-pattern.
distractors.C: A generic community server will not satisfy proprietary schema needs, audit logging, or HIPAA controls, and points untrusted code at sensitive data.
distractors.D: Abandoning MCP forfeits structured, controlled tool access and does not address compliance; manual pasting is error-prone and unauditable.

stem: A single research agent has 17 tools and increasingly picks the wrong one, with slow, token-heavy selection turns. Following the tool-count heuristics, what is the most effective architectural change?

options.A: Merge several tools into a few mega-tools that each take a `mode` parameter
options.B: Partition the 17 tools into specialized subagents of roughly 4-5 tools each, fronted by a coordinator that delegates
options.C: Add a few-shot example for each of the 17 tools to teach correct selection
options.D: Since the count is well under 10, simply rewrite the descriptions to be more distinct

explanation: Once a tool set grows past ~15, the right move is to PARTITION tools across specialized subagents (about 4-5 tools each) with a coordinator delegating to them. Fixing descriptions is the remedy when the count is under ~10; at 17 tools, description tweaks alone will not overcome attention dilution and tool-space interference.

distractors.A: Mega-tools with a `mode` flag hide capability behind opaque parameters, harming selection clarity rather than improving it.
distractors.C: Adding 17 few-shot examples bloats context further and does not address the underlying scaling problem.
distractors.D: 17 is past the partition threshold, not under 10; description rewrites help with small sets but will not resolve degradation at this scale.

stem: Three MCP tools each return data with different date formats: `get_customer` uses Unix timestamps, `lookup_order` uses ISO 8601, and `process_refund` returns numeric status codes. The agent occasionally misreads them. You cannot modify the upstream MCP servers. What is the best client-side fix?

options.A: Switch to a larger model that is better at parsing mixed formats
options.B: Add a paragraph to the system prompt explaining how to interpret each format
options.C: Add several few-shot examples demonstrating correct interpretation of each format
options.D: Use a PostToolUse hook to deterministically normalize each tool's output into consistent formats before the model sees it

explanation: A PostToolUse hook intercepts tool results and transforms them deterministically before the model processes them — ideal for normalizing inconsistent output formats without touching the MCP servers. Prompt-based and few-shot approaches are probabilistic; a model swap does not address the structural inconsistency.

distractors.A: A larger model is costly and still relies on probabilistic parsing rather than guaranteeing consistent formats.
distractors.B: System-prompt instructions are probabilistic and add ongoing token overhead while still leaving room for misinterpretation.
distractors.C: Few-shot examples reduce but do not eliminate errors and consume context every turn; normalization should be deterministic.

stem: Your `lookup_order` tool returns 42 fields per order, but the agent needs only 5 for return processing. Across long conversations the verbose results bloat context. You cannot change the MCP server, and the trimming logic is purely mechanical (keep a fixed field subset). What is the most appropriate mechanism?

options.A: A PostToolUse hook that trims each `lookup_order` result to the 5 needed fields before it enters context
options.B: Raising max_tokens so the verbose results fit
options.C: Asking customers to read order details aloud to avoid the tool call
options.D: Instructions in CLAUDE.md telling the model to ignore the unused 37 fields

explanation: Trimming a known, fixed subset of fields is deterministic mechanical work — exactly what a PostToolUse hook is for. It strips the unused fields before the result accumulates in context, preventing bloat without modifying the server. Prompt instructions still leave the full payload in context; raising token limits does not stop the waste.

distractors.B: Increasing max_tokens accommodates the waste instead of eliminating it and worsens cost and dilution.
distractors.C: Pushing data entry onto customers degrades the experience and abandons the tool's value.
distractors.D: Telling the model to ignore fields still loads all 42 into context every turn, so the bloat persists.

stem: In your repo, Claude Code consistently reaches for the built-in Grep tool instead of your custom `search_internal_kb` MCP tool, even when the answer lives in the internal knowledge base. The MCP tool works when invoked manually. What is the best first remedy?

options.A: Lower the model temperature to make it favor MCP tools
options.B: Remove the built-in Grep tool so the model is forced to use the MCP tool
options.C: Move the MCP server config into CLAUDE.md so the model notices it
options.D: Enhance the `search_internal_kb` description to clearly explain its capabilities, what it returns, and when to prefer it over file-content search

explanation: When the model prefers a built-in tool over an MCP tool, the usual cause is that the MCP tool's description does not make its distinct capabilities and outputs clear. Enhancing the description (what it does, what it returns, when to use it over alternatives) steers selection toward it.

distractors.A: Temperature does not change which tool semantically fits; it will not redirect selection to the MCP tool.
distractors.B: Removing a useful built-in tool is heavy-handed and breaks legitimate file-content search; the real issue is description clarity.
distractors.C: MCP server configuration belongs in `.mcp.json`/`~/.claude.json`, not CLAUDE.md, and placement there does not influence tool-selection.

stem: A reviewer argues: "We set `readOnlyHint: true` and `destructiveHint: false` on our `cancel_subscription` tool, so the host can safely auto-approve it without user confirmation." Why is this reasoning flawed?

options.A: Annotations only apply to resources, so they have no bearing on tool approval at all
options.B: The flaw is only that `readOnlyHint` should have been false; with that one change, auto-approval would be safe
options.C: It is sound; annotations are authoritative and hosts should trust them for auto-approval
options.D: Annotations are untrusted hints; the host MUST NOT make security-critical decisions based solely on them and should still enforce its own controls

explanation: Tool annotations are HINTS, not guarantees. The MCP spec states clients/hosts must not make security-critical decisions based solely on annotations, since a server could advertise misleading values. The host must enforce its own authorization and confirmation policies regardless of what a tool claims.

distractors.A: Annotations apply to tools (readOnly/destructive/idempotent/openWorld hints); the claim that they only apply to resources is incorrect.
distractors.B: Even with perfectly accurate annotations, the host still cannot delegate security-critical approval to them; correcting one value does not make sole-reliance safe.
distractors.C: Annotations are explicitly non-authoritative hints; trusting them for security decisions is exactly what the spec warns against.

stem: You want to guarantee that `validate_identity` runs before `process_refund` is ever callable, on every conversation, with no exceptions. A teammate proposes combining them into one tool; another proposes a forced `tool_choice`; another proposes a hook. Which gives the strongest, deterministic guarantee that verification is enforced?

options.A: Use `tool_choice: {type: tool, name: validate_identity}` on the first turn so identity is checked first
options.B: Add an emphatic system-prompt rule that the model must always verify before refunding
options.C: Implement a pre-tool-use hook that blocks `process_refund` unless `validate_identity` has already succeeded in the session
options.D: Combine both into a single `validate_and_refund` tool so verification and refund are inseparable

explanation: When a business rule requires guaranteed compliance, a hook provides deterministic enforcement the model cannot bypass. A pre-tool-use hook that blocks `process_refund` until `validate_identity` has succeeded enforces the ordering regardless of model behavior. Forced tool_choice and prompt rules are model-side and probabilistic; tool-combination can work but couples concerns and forces validation even when it is already done.

distractors.A: Forced tool_choice only steers the first turn and does not prevent the model from later calling `process_refund` without re-verification; it is not a hard guarantee.
distractors.B: System-prompt rules are probabilistic and known to be skipped a fraction of the time, so they cannot guarantee compliance.
distractors.D: Merging the tools couples two distinct concerns, prevents reusing verification independently, and re-runs validation on every refund even when identity was already confirmed.

stem: A subagent's `extract_fields` tool encounters three distinct failures while processing a batch: a rate-limit (HTTP 429) from the source API, a malformed input record, and an access-denied (403) on one protected document. To enable the best coordinator recovery, how should these be categorized?

options.A: Rate-limit and access-denied as `transient`/retryable, malformed input as `permission`
options.B: All three as `validation` errors, since each involves bad data of some kind
options.C: All three as `transient` with `isRetryable: true`, since retrying is the simplest uniform policy
options.D: Rate-limit as `transient`/retryable, malformed input as `validation`/not-retryable, access-denied as `permission`/not-retryable

explanation: Each failure maps to a distinct error category with a distinct recovery: a 429 rate-limit is `transient` and retryable (back off and retry); a malformed record is a `validation` error, not retryable (needs different input); a 403 access-denied is a `permission` error, not retryable (needs different credentials). Accurate categorization lets the coordinator retry only what is retryable and route the rest appropriately.

distractors.A: Access-denied is a permission failure, not transient, and a malformed record is a validation failure, not permission; this swaps two categories.
distractors.B: A rate-limit and an access-denied are not validation problems; calling them validation hides their true (retryable vs credential) nature.
distractors.C: Uniformly marking everything transient causes endless useless retries of the malformed record and the access-denied document, which will never succeed on retry.

stem: Your repo has a project-scoped `github` MCP server in committed `.mcp.json`. A developer needs to temporarily point their own session at a forked, experimental GitHub MCP endpoint without changing the shared file or affecting teammates. Given the scope precedence (local > project > user), what should they do?

options.A: Add a user-scoped `github` server; user scope overrides project scope globally
options.B: Add a local-scoped `github` server entry pointing at the experimental endpoint; local precedence overrides the project entry for that developer only
options.C: Edit the committed `.mcp.json` to the experimental URL and revert before pushing
options.D: Delete the project entry from `.mcp.json` so the experimental server is the only one

explanation: Local scope is private to the developer and the project, and has the highest precedence (local > project > user). Adding a local-scoped `github` entry overrides the shared project entry only for that developer, leaving the committed `.mcp.json` and teammates untouched.

distractors.A: User scope is the LOWEST precedence and is overridden by the project entry, so it would not take effect over the committed `github` server.
distractors.C: Editing the shared committed file risks accidentally pushing the experimental URL and affects everyone until reverted.
distractors.D: Deleting the project entry modifies the shared file and breaks the server for all teammates.

stem: You gave the document-analysis agent a general-purpose `fetch_url` tool so it could download documents by URL. Production logs show this agent now frequently downloads search-engine results pages to perform ad hoc web search — behavior that should be routed through the web-search agent — causing inconsistent results. Which fix is most effective?

options.A: Replace `fetch_url` with a `load_document` tool that validates that URLs point to document formats
options.B: Remove `fetch_url` from the document-analysis agent and route all URL fetching through the coordinator to the web-search agent
options.C: Implement filtering that blocks `fetch_url` calls to known search-engine domains while allowing other URLs
options.D: Add instructions to the document-analysis agent's prompt that `fetch_url` should only be used to download document URLs, not to search

explanation: Replacing the general-purpose tool with a document-specific `load_document` tool that validates URL targets constrains capability at the interface level. This follows the principle of least privilege: the undesired search behavior becomes impossible rather than merely discouraged, while the agent keeps the document-download capability it legitimately needs.

distractors.B: Removing the tool entirely takes away a capability the agent legitimately needs (downloading documents by URL), forcing extra coordinator round trips for its core workflow.
distractors.C: A domain blocklist is brittle and incomplete — new or obscure search endpoints bypass it, so the misuse persists.
distractors.D: Prompt instructions are probabilistic; the over-broad capability remains available, so misuse is discouraged but not prevented.

stem: Production logs show a consistent pattern: when customers include the word "account" in their message (e.g., "I want to check my account for an order I made yesterday"), the agent calls `get_customer` first 78% of the time; when customers phrase similar requests without "account" (e.g., "I want to check an order I made yesterday"), it calls `lookup_order` first 93% of the time. Tool descriptions are clear and unambiguous. What is the most likely root cause of this discrepancy?

options.A: The system prompt contains keyword-sensitive instructions that steer behavior based on terms like "account," creating unintended tool-selection patterns.
options.B: The model's base training creates associations between "account" terminology and customer-related operations that override the tool descriptions.
options.C: The model needs more training data on multi-concept messages and should be fine-tuned on examples containing both account and order terminology.
options.D: The tool descriptions need additional negative examples specifying when NOT to use each tool, to prevent this keyword-induced confusion.

explanation: A split this sharp and systematic (78% vs 93%, conditioned on a single keyword) points to explicit routing logic somewhere in the prompt reacting to the word "account" and steering the agent toward customer-related tools. Since the tool descriptions are stated to be clear, the prompt's own instructions are the most likely source of the unintended steering.

distractors.B: Base-training associations produce diffuse tendencies, not the sharp, systematic keyword-conditioned split observed here; a pattern this clean indicates explicit instructions.
distractors.C: Fine-tuning is not how tool selection is steered in this architecture, and the behavior is far too systematic to be a training-data coverage gap.
distractors.D: Adding negative examples to descriptions treats the symptom; the stem states the descriptions are already clear, so the steering must originate elsewhere — in the system prompt.

stem: Your `remove_team_member` tool uses a `dry_run: boolean` parameter so impacts can be previewed before execution, but production monitoring shows the agent bypasses the preview step by calling with `dry_run=false` directly. You need to guarantee that every removal is preceded by a preview that the user explicitly confirms. What is the most reliable approach?

options.A: Add server-side validation that permits `dry_run=false` only when a `dry_run=true` call with identical parameters occurred within the past 60 seconds.
options.B: Annotate the tool as requiring confirmation and configure the orchestration layer to prompt the user for approval before forwarding calls to annotated tools.
options.C: Add detailed instructions and few-shot examples to the tool description requiring the agent to always call with `dry_run=true` first and wait for user confirmation before calling again.
options.D: Split it into two tools: `preview_remove_member` returns the impact details plus a single-use confirmation token, and `execute_remove_member` requires that token, structurally binding execution to a prior preview.

explanation: The two-tool token-binding design makes it architecturally impossible to execute a removal without a prior preview: the execute tool requires a single-use token that only the preview tool can mint. The constraint is enforced in code at the tool boundary rather than depending on LLM compliance, timing heuristics, or host-side cooperation.

distractors.A: A 60-second timing window is a heuristic, not a guarantee — the agent can fire a preview and immediately execute without any user confirmation, and legitimate user confirmations can also take longer than 60 seconds.
distractors.B: Annotations are advisory hints; hosts are not obligated to honor them, so this moves the guarantee outside the tool design into infrastructure you may not control.
distractors.C: Description-level instructions depend entirely on LLM compliance — exactly the mechanism already failing in production.

stem: Production monitoring shows your `search_catalog` tool fails 12% of the time: 8% are network timeouts that succeed when retried, and 4% are query syntax errors that never succeed regardless of retries. Both error types are currently returned identically, causing the agent to waste retries on unfixable errors. How should you modify the tool's error handling?

options.A: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating how to distinguish network errors from syntax errors.
options.B: Apply exponential-backoff retry logic uniformly to all errors.
options.C: Implement automatic retry with backoff for network timeouts inside the tool itself, and return syntax errors immediately with parameter-validation details the agent can act on.
options.D: Return all errors with a `retryable` boolean flag plus error-type details, and let the agent decide whether to retry.

explanation: Retrying transient failures is deterministic plumbing the tool can handle itself — it has definitive knowledge of the error type. Handling timeouts internally and returning syntax errors immediately with actionable validation details puts each error at the correct abstraction boundary: mechanical retries in code, semantic correction (fixing the query) with the model.

distractors.A: Few-shot examples ask the LLM to classify errors the tool can already distinguish deterministically; this places the logic at the wrong abstraction boundary.
distractors.B: Uniform backoff retries syntax errors that can never succeed, adding latency and cost on the 4% slice that needs a different fix entirely.
distractors.D: A `retryable` flag is better than undifferentiated errors but still delegates mechanical retry behavior to the agent, which may not follow it consistently; transient retries are better handled deterministically inside the tool.
